title: Formerly CODEX STAN C-4-1966. Adopted in 1966. Revised in 2007.

sections.section: Formerly CODEX STAN C-4-1966. Adopted in 1966. Revised in 2007.
sections.summary: Document history noting this was formerly CODEX STAN C-4-1966, adopted in 1966 and revised in 2007.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR EDAM CXS 265-1966 Amended in 2008, 2010, 2013, 2018, 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Title: Standard for Edam (CXS 265-1966) with amendments recorded in 2008, 2010, 2013, 2018, 2019 and 2022.

2022 amendment: amended the previous labelling-of-non-retail-containers text so that labelling of non-retail containers should be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Scope: the standard applies to Edam intended for direct consumption or further processing; Description: Edam is a ripened firm/semi-hard cheese with characteristic colour, firm cutting texture, typical small gas holes and spherical/flat block/loaf shapes (with or without rind), and ripening for consumption is normally about three weeks at 10–18 °C though alternative conditions producing equivalent properties are permitted.

Edam is a ripened firm/semi-hard cheese with near white to yellow colour, firm texture suitable for cutting, typically small round gas holes up to about 10 mm, sold in spherical, flat block or loaf shapes with dry rind (or rindless), and ripening for ready-to-eat Edam is normally about three weeks at 10–18 °C though equivalent alternative ripening conditions are acceptable.

Essential composition and quality factors: raw materials are cow's or buffalo's milk (or mixtures); permitted ingredients include harmless starter cultures, rennet/coagulating enzymes, salt and substitutes, potable water, ripening enzymes, processing aids and limited surface anti‑caking agents; compositional requirements include a minimum milk fat in dry matter of 30% and specified minimum dry matter levels tied to fat-in-dry-matter bands, and deviations beyond the specified minima/maxima are not in compliance with the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms.
Specifies permitted raw materials as cows' milk, buffaloes' milk, or their mixtures and products derived from these milks.

Lists permitted ingredients: harmless lactic/flavour-producing starter cultures and other harmless micro-organisms, rennet or other safe coagulating enzymes, sodium chloride and potassium chloride as salt substitutes, potable water, safe ripening enzymes, safe processing aids, and allows rice/corn/potato flours and starches as surface anti-caking agents for cut/sliced/shredded products only in amounts functionally necessary under GMP; includes a footnote on rindless cheese and use of ripening film/coating.

Sets compositional requirements: milk fat in dry matter minimum 30% (no maximum; reference 40–50%), and specifies minimum dry matter by fat-in-dry-matter bands (≥30<40 → 47%; ≥40<45 → 51%; ≥45<50 → 55%; ≥50<60 → 57%; ≥60 → 62%); deviations beyond these minima/maxima are not in compliance with Section 4.3.3 of CXS 206-1999.

Permits only additive functional classes justified in the accompanying table and in accordance with the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995), allowing certain colours, acidity regulators, preservatives (in mass and/or surface as specified) and surface anti-caking agents as indicated.

Requires products and the milk used in their manufacture to comply with maximum levels for contaminants and toxins in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with CAC-established maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides in milk.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and compliance with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.

Adds specific labelling requirements to the general labelling and dairy terms standards, covering permitted use of the name (Edam/variants only if conforming), required country-of-origin declaration, how milk fat must be declared, date-marking options, and labelling of non-retail containers.

Allows the names Edam, Edamer or Edammer only for cheeses that conform to this Standard (alternative spellings permitted locally), requires products with fat outside the reference range but above the absolute minimum to carry a qualifying description or fat declaration, and permits the designation for cut, sliced, shredded or grated products made from conforming cheese.
Requires declaration of the country of origin defined as the country of manufacture, with the country of substantial transformation treated as the origin for labelling purposes.

Requires the milk fat content to be declared in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale: as percentage by mass, percentage of fat in dry matter, or grams per serving (with number of servings stated).
Permits declaring the date of manufacture instead of minimum durability information provided the product is not intended to be purchased as such by the final consumer.
Advises that labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
States that methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to this standard shall be used to check compliance.
Informative appendix (not affecting mandatory provisions) giving additional product information: typical spherical Edam weights 1.5–2.5 kg and that salting is by brine.
